narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, along with your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time. Your Year 1 school decile ranking placed you in the bottom tenth, and your Year 3 decile was similarly low at the first percentile, while your LSAT score of 26 falls well below the average of about 37. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 53.4% chance that you would fail the bar—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment to help the school select applicants most likely to thrive in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking, as being in the lowest group within your school signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance through later years. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a strong favorable pull because it exceeded typical levels and suggested solid foundational skills. Your family's upper-middle income also nudged the prediction positively, reflecting resources that often support academic success. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower Year 3 decile stands out as a bigger drag than average, while your GPA gives you an edge many lack. Overall, these top factors created a close call, with risks outweighing strengths just enough for denial. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the norm, strengthening later-year grades if possible, or seeking targeted prep programs that have helped many Hispanic students like you excel on the bar. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also sway human reviewers in future applications. With focused improvements, you're positioned to reapply stronger next cycle.
